material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel and gilt decoration
description:
This vase depicts the Sui dynasty emperor Yangdi aboard his dragon boat. Yangdi, who ruled from 604 to around 618, sponsored the building of China’s Grand Canal, which linked the populous north with the fertile south. 


The scene here might be a comment on the excesses of rulership and power: Yangdi’s enormous project entailed the conscription of more than two million people. Not surprisingly, his rule ended with a revolution.
credit_line: Purchased with Museum funds, 1876
material_zh: 釉上瓷釉和镀金装饰的瓷器
description_zh:
这个花瓶描绘了隋朝皇帝杨棣乘坐龙舟的情景。公元604年至618年左右统治中国的杨迪，赞助修建了连接人口稠密的北方和富饶的南方的大运河。
这里的场景可能是对统治和权力过度的评论：杨迪的庞大项目需要征召200多万人。毫不奇怪，他的统治以革命告终。p

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: Part of a dinner service, this plate bears the coat of arms of Anders Bachmansson (1697–1772), who was ennobled under the name Nordencrantz in 1743. Bachmansson was a trade councilor and factory owner. Decorative elements in the border—rods, anvils, waterwheels, and furnaces—make reference to his profession; the shell and bees are taken from his coat of arms; and the bear is from his crest.
credit_line: The Bloomfield Moore Collection, 1882
description_zh: 作为晚餐服务的一部分，这个盘子上有安德斯·巴赫曼森（1697-1772）的徽章，他于1743年以诺登朗茨的名义被授予爵位。巴赫曼森是一名贸易委员和工厂主。边框上的装饰元素——杆子、铁砧、水车和炉子——都与他的职业有关；贝壳和蜜蜂都是从他的纹章上取下来的；熊是从它的头顶上长出来的。

material: Wood (willow) with traces of paint, stone
description:
It is likely this serene bodhisattva was made for a Buddhist temple in northern China. It probably flanked a large Buddha statue set against a mural painting depicting paradise. 


Sculpted in high relief, the deity has inlaid glazed ceramic eyes and traces of its original brightly colored painted decoration and gilding. It also has a cavity in its back that would have been filled with devotional items. Meant to ensure the potency of the statue, the holy offerings included prayers and colored stones representing the heart and other organs.
credit_line: Gift of Charles H. Ludington from the George Crofts Collection, 1925
material_zh: 有油漆、石头痕迹的木头（柳树）
description_zh:
这尊宁静的菩萨很可能是为中国北方的一座佛教寺庙而造的。它可能侧面是一尊巨大的佛像，旁边是一幅描绘天堂的壁画。
神像以高浮雕雕刻而成，镶嵌着上釉陶瓷眼，以及其原始色彩鲜艳的装饰和镀金痕迹。它的背部也有一个空腔，里面装满了虔诚的物品。为了确保雕像的效力，圣物包括祈祷和代表心脏和其他器官的彩色石头。p

material: Ink on silk; mounted as a hanging scroll, framed
description:
Known as the fearless captor of ghosts and evil spirits in both China and Japan, Zhong Kui is one of the most frequently portrayed figures of Chinese folklore. Supernatural stories about Zhong appear early on in the Song dynasty (960–1279), where it is accounted that he subjugated two demons for Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang dynasty (618–906). Based on this legend, portraits of Zhong are hung during the Lunar New Year to help expel evil spirits.

This painting shows the stout Zhong Kui wearing ceremonial attire of an official, holding a stack of books in one hand and carrying a stringed instrument under his other arm. This is most likely intended to demonstrate the cultural refinement of this boorish-looking warrior, who, as the legend relates, once achieved top rank in the civil service examination.
credit_line: Gift of Charles H. Ludington, 1925
material_zh: 丝绸上的墨水；装裱成悬挂的卷轴
description_zh: 钟馗是中国民间传说中最常被描绘的人物之一，在中国和日本都被称为无所畏惧的捉鬼捉鬼者。关于钟的超自然故事早在宋代（960-1279）就出现了，据说他为唐玄宗（618-906）征服了两个恶魔。根据这个传说，钟的肖像在农历新年期间悬挂，以帮助驱除邪灵。这幅画描绘了身材魁梧的钟馗身着官服，一手拿着一堆书，另一只胳膊下夹着一把弦乐器。这很可能是为了展示这位长相粗鲁的战士的文化修养，正如传说所说，他曾在公务员考试中获得过最高级别。

material: Limestone
description: This grotesque head once hung inside a tomb chamber, probably as a guardian to protect the deceased. It is rendered in a primitive manner with basic geometric elements that create a fearsome and mystic image. This guardian’s bold facial features are precisely executed with a mix of relief and incised lines on a curved surface, which attest to a studied handling by an experienced carver.
credit_line: Purchased with Museum and Subscription funds, 1927
description_zh: 这个怪诞的头曾经挂在墓室里，可能是作为保护死者的监护人。它以原始的方式呈现，具有基本的几何元素，创造了一个可怕而神秘的形象。这位守护者大胆的面部特征是通过在曲面上混合浮雕和切割线精确地完成的，这证明了经验丰富的雕刻师经过精心研究的处理。

material: Marble with traces of painted decoration
description: Luohans, or arhats, are disciples of the Buddha and guardians of Buddhist Law. They are enlightened beings, no longer bounded by the cycle of reincarnation. Since the Northern Song dynasty, certain luohans began to be portrayed with a tamed tiger, possibly as a token of their supernatural powers.
credit_line: Gift of Benjamin R. Hoffman, 1927
material_zh: 带有彩绘装饰痕迹的大理石
description_zh: 罗汉，或称罗汉，是佛陀的弟子和佛教律法的守护者。他们是开悟的人，不再受轮回的束缚。自北宋以来，某些罗汉开始被描绘成驯服的老虎，这可能是他们超自然力量的象征。

description: According to the Chinese Buddhist tradition, Weituo is a guardian deity credited with fighting off demons that stole the relics of the Buddha. He usually sports the appearance of a general, donning a tall helmet and a full suit of golden armor, secured at the waist with a belt adorned with a demon mask. Here Weituo holds a jeweled sword across both arms, a symbolic weapon that indicates his duty to guard the Buddhist Law.
credit_line: Purchased with funds contributed by Edward B. Robinette from the Simkhovitch Collection, 1929
material_zh: 丝绸上的墨水和颜色；被陷害的
description_zh: 根据中国佛教传统，威陀是一位守护神，被认为可以击退偷走佛陀遗物的恶魔。他通常有将军的样子，戴着高高的头盔和全套金色盔甲，腰间系着一条装饰着恶魔面具的腰带。在这里，魏陀双手持一把宝石剑，这是一种象征性的武器，表明他有义务遵守佛教律法。

material: Ink and color on silk; fan mounted as an album leaf
description: The image of the solitary lady is drawn from the final couplet of the poem "Jiaren" (Fair Lady), composed by the celebrated Tang poet Du Fu (712–770): "The weather is cold, her azure sleeves are thin, At twilight she rests by tall bamboo." The pale ink washes that make up the background of this painting indicate the approach of dusk, and the four tall, thin bamboo branches connecting the upper and lower edges of the painting provide the rest of the poem’s setting. In between a pierced Taihu rock and the tall bamboo is a plum tree with a profusion of white blossoms. Together with the camellias at the lower right, they indicate that the cold winter night is coming. The grouping of plum blossom, bamboo, and rock is called the Three Purities (san qing), echoing the character of the lady who is the embodiment of surpassing beauty.
dimensions:
Image: 9 3/8 x 9 1/8 inches (23.8 x 23.2 cm)
Mount: 12 5/8 x 13 5/8 inches (32.1 x 34.6 cm)
credit_line: Purchased with Museum Funds from the Simkhovitch Collection, 1929
material_zh: 丝绸上的墨水和颜色；风扇装成相册的叶子
description_zh: 孤女形象取自唐代著名诗人杜甫（712-770）的诗《美人》的最后一副对联：“天冷，碧蓝的衣袖薄，黄昏时，她靠着高大的竹子休息。”这幅画的背景是淡水墨，预示着黄昏的临近，连接画作上下边缘的四根又高又细的竹枝构成了这首诗的其余背景。在一块被刺穿的太湖岩石和高大的竹子之间，有一棵开满白色花朵的李树。与右下角的山茶花一起，它们表明寒冷的冬夜即将到来。梅花、竹子和岩石的组合被称为三清，呼应了超越美的化身——淑女的性格。

material: Ink and color on silk; fan mounted as an album leaf
description: Three female immortals wear cloaks and skirts made of grasses and leaves. Their hairy bodies identify them as the Feathered Women (mao nü) of Daoist lore, who live deep in the mountains searching for herbs. Here they show each other what they have gathered. The figure at the far left holds a lingzhi fungus (thought to bestow longevity if ingested) and a basket of plants is draped over her arm. The focus of the painting is on a large health-benefiting ginseng root displayed in the hand of the central immortal. Other herbs depicted include leaves of banana and palm tree carried in the baskets on the women’s backs. The representation of Feathered Women exhibiting precious medicinal items functions as a special talisman, protecting the owner from illness.
credit_line: Purchased with Museum funds from the Simkhovitch Collection, 1929
material_zh: 丝绸上的墨水和颜色；风扇装成相册的叶子
description_zh: 三位女神仙穿着草叶制成的斗篷和裙子。他们毛茸茸的身体表明他们是道教传说中的羽毛女人（mao nü），她们住在深山中寻找草药。在这里，他们互相展示了他们收集到的东西。最左边的人物拿着灵芝（被认为摄入后可以延年益寿），她的手臂上挂着一篮植物。这幅画的重点是展示在中央神仙手中的一个大的有益健康的人参根。描绘的其他草药包括香蕉叶和棕榈树叶，放在妇女背上的篮子里。展出珍贵药品的羽毛妇女的代表具有特殊的护身符作用，可以保护主人免受疾病。

title_zh: 中式正式花园中的人物茶几
title_en: Tea Table with Figures in a Chinese Formal Garden
material: Wood with lacquer and gilded decoration
description:
Highly refined lacquer furniture—generally in the form of chests or cabinets made in China and Japan for their domestic markets—was available, in limited quantities, in Europe from as early as the mid-seventeenth century. These costly and cherished objects were found in many of Europe’s most prestigious households, where they were often raised on stands with elaborate gilded or japanned (the European version of lacquer) decoration. 

By the early nineteenth century, the fashion for Asian lacquerware had reached the middle classes in Europe and the United States. In response, Asian craftsmen began to manufacture Western-style lacquer furniture such as this example. This table was made for Philadelphian John A. Lewis while he was in China. Lewis’s father was in the China trade and when he died, John and his brother took over the business, which was located at the southeast corner of Walnut and Front Streets in Philadelphia.
credit_line: Gift of Mrs. Joseph H. Burroughs, 1940
material_zh: 带漆和镀金装饰的木材
description_zh: 早在17世纪中叶，欧洲就有数量有限的高度精制的漆器家具，通常是中国和日本为国内市场制造的箱子或橱柜。这些昂贵而珍贵的物品在欧洲许多最负盛名的家庭中都有发现，在那里，它们经常被放在有精美镀金或日本（欧洲版的漆器）装饰的架子上。到19世纪初，亚洲漆器的时尚已经到达了欧洲和美国的中产阶级。作为回应，亚洲工匠开始制造像这样的西式漆器家具。这张桌子是为费城人约翰·A·刘易斯在中国时做的。刘易斯的父亲从事中国贸易，他去世时，约翰和他的兄弟接管了这家位于费城核桃街和前街东南角的公司。

material: Lacquered wood with gilt decoration; brass fittings
description:
During the Qing dynasty the depiction of this particular type of dragon—with five claws—was restricted to imperial items. Add in the furniture’s impressive size and the sumptuousness of the gold decoration and it is clear this wardrobe once belonged to a high-ranking Chinese official or nobleman. It would have been placed both in the private areas of residences and in public spaces like a reception hall.


The bottom sections stored silk garments while those at the top, accessed by a ladder, held smaller items such as hats.
dimensions: 9 feet 6 1/4 inches × 4 feet 8 1/2 inches × 2 feet 2 5/16 inches (290.2 × 143.5 × 66.8 cm)
credit_line: Purchased with the Bloomfield Moore Fund, 1940
material_zh: 镀金装饰的漆木；铜配件
description_zh:
在清朝，这种特殊类型的龙——有五只爪子——的描绘仅限于皇室物品。再加上家具令人印象深刻的尺寸和金色装饰的奢华，很明显这个衣柜曾经属于一位中国高级官员或贵族。它将被放置在住宅的私人区域和接待大厅等公共空间。
底部存放丝绸服装，而顶部通过梯子进入，存放帽子等较小的物品。p

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration
description: The image in the center panel of this bowl derives from the Saga of Chu and Han. Zhang Liang, a loyal retainer of Liu Bang, the first emperor of the Han dynasty (206 B.C.-A.D. 220), picks up a shoe for a Daoist Immortal disguised as an old man. Zhang was later rewarded with a secret military book for this good deed.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944
description_zh: 这个碗中央的图像来源于楚汉传奇。汉朝（公元前206年-公元220年）第一位皇帝刘邦的忠实侍从张亮为一位伪装成老人的道教神仙捡了一只鞋。张后来因这一善举获得了一本秘密军事书籍作为奖励。

material: Carved pink glass
description: Glassmaking has been practiced in China for centuries, but was never accorded the honor or appreciation given to ceramics. Beginning in the Tang dynasty (618-907), glass was used as a cheaper substitute for jade. In the Qing dynasty, semiopaque glasses of various colors were used to imitate various kinds of hard stones, such as the pink jade of which this piece is reminiscent.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944
description_zh: 玻璃制造在中国已经存在了几个世纪，但从未被赋予陶瓷那样的荣誉或欣赏。从唐朝（618-907）开始，玻璃被用作玉石的廉价替代品。在清朝，人们用各种颜色的半透明玻璃来模仿各种坚硬的石头，比如这件作品让人联想到的粉红色玉石。

material: Opaque white glass with enamel decoration; green and brown glass stopper with ivory spoon
description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944
material_zh: 带有珐琅装饰的不透明白色玻璃；绿棕色玻璃塞，带象牙勺
description_zh:
17世纪末，由烟草磨成粉末并加入芳香物质制成的鼻烟从日本传入中国。中国精英认为这种粉末具有药用价值，最初使用圆柱形药瓶来装这种新的“日本烟草”。不久之后，康熙皇帝（1662-1722年在位）在北京建立了一系列作坊，生产供宫廷使用的小型优质物品，包括鼻烟壶。在帝国的赞助下，瓶子形状、材料和图案的种类急剧增加，工匠们通过添加带有象牙勺的塞子来方便烟草的分配。
在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。P

material: Wood (zitan)
description:
This six-sided stool is made out of a precious hardwood—called zitan in China—used primarily for furniture gracing Qing dynasty palaces. This example convey auspicious messages through the fruits and flowers held by the pairs of young boys on each side. 


Auspicious Decoration: Flower and Fruits 


Flower: lotus for harmony. Fruits: pomegranate and lychee for many sons, Buddha’s Hand and peach for longevity, and persimmon for a successful career.


Overall, the design functions as a rebus (a visual riddle) offering wishes for many happy, wealthy, and long-lived children.
credit_line: Gift of Mrs. John Story Jenks, 1946
description_zh:
这种六面凳是由一种珍贵的硬木制成的，在中国被称为紫檀，主要用于清朝宫殿的家具。这个例子通过两边成对的小男孩拿着的水果和鲜花传达吉祥的信息。
吉祥装饰：花果
花：和谐的莲花。水果：石榴和荔枝为多子，佛手和桃子为长寿，柿子为事业成功。
总的来说，这个设计就像一个视觉谜语，为许多幸福、富有、长寿的孩子送上祝福。p

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration
description: The flowering plum is one of the most beloved and enduring images in Chinese art, in part because it is among the first plants to bloom each spring. As a sixth-century poet noted, "She alone has the gift of recognizing spring." From the twelfth century on, an entire genre of painting was devoted to aspects of the flowering plum. On this spectacular vase, the artist has given us one interpretation: a night view with the tree set against the dark, black sky. The delicate blossoms contrast with the gnarled, aged trunk of the tree as both wrap around the contours of the perfectly formed vase.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
description_zh: 梅花是中国艺术中最受欢迎和最持久的形象之一，部分原因是它是每年春天最早开花的植物之一。正如六世纪的一位诗人所说，“只有她有识春的天赋。”从十二世纪开始，整个绘画流派都致力于梅花的各个方面。在这个壮观的花瓶上，艺术家给了我们一个解释：在黑暗的夜空中，树木映衬着黑暗的天空。精致的花朵与粗糙的老树干形成鲜明对比，两者都环绕着形状完美的花瓶的轮廓。

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration (Famille rose)
description:
This delicate porcelain lantern is so light in the hand that it feels almost weightless. Candles placed within it would have glowed and flickered through the intricate lattice designs, which required great skill to achieve due to the extremely thin clay. It was not until the early 1700s that artists developed this piercing technique.


Auspicious Decoration 


The four flowers within the central medallions on the six sides—the camellia, rose, peony, and lily—convey peace, unity, and longevity.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
material_zh: 釉上搪瓷装饰瓷器（粉彩玫瑰）
description_zh:
这个精致的瓷灯笼拿在手里很轻，感觉几乎没有重量。放在里面的蜡烛会在复杂的格子设计中闪闪发光，由于粘土非常薄，这需要高超的技巧才能实现。直到18世纪初，艺术家们才开发出这种穿孔技术。
吉祥装饰
六面中央奖章内的四朵花——山茶花、玫瑰、牡丹和百合——传达着和平、团结和长寿。p

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration (Famille noire)
description: On this dramatic black vase a pheasant perched on a rock seems to call to its mate while a single butterfly flutters above blooming peonies, magnolias, and plum blossoms. The floral patterns continue harmoniously on the curved surfaces of the neck and lid, demonstrating the masterful skill of the Chinese porcelain painter.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
material_zh: 釉上搪瓷装饰瓷器（黑色家族）
description_zh: 在这个戏剧性的黑色花瓶上，一只栖息在岩石上的野鸡似乎在呼唤它的伴侣，而一只蝴蝶在盛开的牡丹、木兰和梅花上方飞舞。花朵图案在颈部和盖子的曲面上和谐地延续，展示了中国瓷器画家的精湛技艺。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with underglaze blue and enamel decoration
description: While the decoration on this beaker, known in Europe as famille verte (green family), is based on that of Chinese porcelain of the Kangxi period (1662–1722), its shape is modeled on a European drinking vessel, perhaps one in glass that was shipped to China by European traders for the purpose of being copied in porcelain.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
material_zh: 釉下蓝珐琅装饰硬质瓷
description_zh: 虽然这个烧杯上的装饰在欧洲被称为famille verte（绿色家族），是以康熙时期（1662-1722）的中国瓷器为基础的，但它的形状是仿照一个欧洲饮酒器皿，也许是一个玻璃器皿，由欧洲商人运往中国，目的是在瓷器上复制。

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration (Famille jaune)
description: Famille jaune is the name given by European collectors to Chinese wares with a yellow ground and painted motifs in green and aubergine, as seen on this vase. Each of its four sides represents one of the four seasons: peony for spring, lotus for summer, chrysanthemum for autumn, and prunus for winter.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
material_zh: 釉上搪瓷装饰瓷器（Famille jaune）
description_zh: Famille jaune是欧洲收藏家对中国瓷器的称呼，这些瓷器有黄色的底色和绿色和茄子的图案，正如这个花瓶上看到的那样。它的四面分别代表四季：春天牡丹，夏天荷花，秋天菊花，冬天李。

title_en: Ewer with Lion-Shaped Finial and Peonies
material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration
description: Chinese potters often took inspiration from other mediums, as they did with this ewer, originally a Middle Eastern metalwork form. The translation into porcelain sets off the exotic handle and spout in a yellow basket-weave pattern. The intricate motif of peonies in aubergine and white simulates an incised pattern on silver. The small lion on the lid is a whimsical, purely Chinese invention.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
description_zh: 中国陶艺家经常从其他媒介中汲取灵感，就像他们用这种最初是中东金属制品形式的水罐一样。将其翻译成瓷器，以黄色篮编织图案衬托出异国情调的手柄和壶嘴。紫红色和白色牡丹的复杂图案模拟了银色上的雕刻图案。盖子上的小狮子是一个异想天开的纯中国发明。

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel decoration (Famille verte)
description: This vase, one of a pair, is decorated with an idyllic garden scene: pheasants perch on rocks amid flowering peonies and magnolias, while blue jays cavort in the branches above. Along the shoulder are the symbols of the "Four Accomplishments" of the Chinese scholar: a pair of scrolls (painting), bound books (calligraphy), a wrapped qin or lute (music), and a game board and pieces (playing xiangqi, a type of Chinese chess game). The auspicious bees, butterflies, and floral sprays at the neck of the vase complete the picture of an ideal scholar's world.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
material_zh: 釉上搪瓷装饰瓷器（Famille verte）
description_zh: 这个花瓶是一对花瓶中的一个，装饰着田园诗般的花园景色：野鸡栖息在盛开的牡丹和木兰花中的岩石上，而蓝鸦在上面的树枝上嬉戏。肩上是中国学者“四成”的象征：一对卷轴（绘画）、一本装订的书（书法）、一把包裹的琴或琵琶（音乐）、一块棋盘和棋子（下棋，一种中国象棋游戏）。花瓶颈上的吉祥蜜蜂、蝴蝶和花香，构成了一幅理想学者世界的画卷。

description:
This festive gathering, which shows ladies in boats picking lotus flowers, still occurs in China today. Beijing hosts a lotus festival each summer, when the pink and white blooms fill lakes with spectacular color. 


To the right, an official and his female companion watch from a pavilion, waited on by attendants and entertained by a small troupe of women musicians.
credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955
description_zh:
这种节日聚会，即船上的女士们采摘莲花，至今仍在中国上演。北京每年夏天都会举办荷花节，届时粉红色和白色的花朵会让湖泊充满壮观的色彩。
在右边，一位官员和他的女性同伴在亭子里观看，由服务员伺候，并由一小群女音乐家招待。p

description: Hollow, cast-iron figures of bodhisattvas that stand more than five feet high have been found in a Buddhist nunnery and several other locations in Henan province. This head was originally part of one such figure. Rectangular holes once used to hold in place an elaborate crown are visible at the base of the protuberance (ushnisa) at the top of the bodhisattva's head.
credit_line: The Samuel S. White 3rd and Vera White Collection, 1967
description_zh: 在河南省的一座佛教尼姑庵和其他几个地方发现了超过五英尺高的空心铸铁菩萨像。这个头最初是一个这样的人物的一部分。在菩萨头顶的突起（ushnisa）底部可以看到曾经用来固定精致王冠的矩形孔。

material: Bronze with gold inlay
description: Official script characters ornament the four sides of the hook and the upper left-hand corner of the base of this delicately designed functional object. Both text and image refer to the Three Kingdoms epic, which features three famous second-century A.D. friends who swore an oath to fight side by side and live and die together. The illustration shows one of them, Kuan Yü, faithfully guarding the two wives of another, Liu Pie.
credit_line: Purchased with Museum funds, 1970
description_zh: 这个设计精巧的功能性物体的钩子的四边和基座的左上角装饰着隶书字符。文字和图像都是指《三国演义》史诗，这部史诗讲述了公元二世纪三位著名的朋友宣誓并肩作战，生死与共的故事。图中，其中一位关羽忠实地守护着另一位刘派的两位妻子。

material: Wood (huanghuali); woven fiber seats
description: During the Ming dynasty, rounded or horseshoe-backed chairs were reserved for honored guests. The gracefully curving crest rail of each of these examples consists of only three pieces of wood connected by highly developed joinery. The vertical rails, made of a single continuous piece of wood, complete the elegant form.
dimensions: 36 x 26 x 29 inches (91.4 x 66 x 73.7 cm)
credit_line: Purchased with the J. Stogdell Stokes Fund, the John T. Morris Fund, the Thomas Skelton Harrison Fund, the George W. B. Taylor Fund, and with funds contributed by Mr. and Mrs. Howard Lewis and Henry B. Keep, 1971
material_zh: 木材（黄花里）；编织纤维座椅
description_zh: 在明朝，圆形或马蹄背的椅子是为贵宾保留的。这些例子中的每一个都是优雅弯曲的顶轨，由高度发达的细木工连接的三块木头组成。由一块连续的木头制成的垂直轨道，完成了优雅的造型。

material: Stoneware with olive-brown glaze
description: The designs of this small headrest demonstrate elements of traditional Chinese architecture intended to ward off malicious influences. A pair of ferocious-looking lions flank an exterior gate with overhanging roofs, which are further guarded by mythical animals. The two sides of this pillow, carved in the form of demon masks, possibly represent the so-called “devil tiles” (guiwa) that often adorn the ends of roof ridges in traditional Chinese architecture.
credit_line: Gift of the Honorable and Mrs. Hugh Scott, 1977
description_zh: 这个小头枕的设计展示了中国传统建筑的元素，旨在抵御恶意影响。一对长相凶猛的狮子站在一扇有悬垂屋顶的外门两侧，这扇外门由神话动物进一步守卫。这个枕头的两侧雕刻着恶魔面具，可能代表了中国传统建筑中经常装饰屋脊末端的所谓“魔鬼瓦”（guiwa）。

material: Jade
description: This elegant, six-lobed cup is made from nephrite jade, a material venerated in China for its ritual significance, association with gentlemanly virtures, and physical properties, holding a place equivalent to that of gold in the West. Connoisseurs of the time appreciated this understated material and celebrated the purity of the form and texture of the stone, here emphasized by the absence of decoration in this cup.
credit_line: Gift of the Far Eastern Art Committee in honor of Henry B. Keep, 1978
description_zh: 这款优雅的六瓣杯由软玉制成，软玉在中国因其仪式意义、与绅士美德的联系和物理特性而备受推崇，在西方的地位相当于黄金。当时的鉴赏家们欣赏这种低调的材料，并庆祝石头的形状和质地的纯净，这里强调的是这个杯子没有装饰。

material: Color, gilding, and ivory on silk; wood, lacquer, and gilt frame; mounted as a twelve-fold screen
description: The ladies in these palace scenes are engaged in pursuits such as looking at antiquities, enjoying plum blossoms in winter, gazing at the moon during the Moon Festival, reading books, dressing hair in the fall, sipping cool soup in the summer, drinking tea, and raising birds and rabbits. Both end panels feature the "Hundred Antiquities," a decorative motif that includes archaic bronzes and other objects popular for a Chinese scholar's study. The feminine scenes and the auspicious creatures with their young on the lower portions of the panels all suggest that this screen was intended for a woman's quarters.
credit_line: Gift of Mrs. Elizabeth K. Van Alen, 1978
material_zh: 丝绸上的颜色、镀金和象牙色；木、漆和镀金框架；安装为十二折屏幕
description_zh: 这些宫景中的女士们从事着诸如看古董、冬天欣赏梅花、中秋节赏月、读书、秋天梳头、夏天喝凉汤、喝茶、养鸟养兔等活动。两块端板上都有“百件古物”，这是一个装饰图案，包括古代青铜器和其他中国学者研究中流行的物品。画面下方的女性场景和带着幼崽的吉祥生物都表明，这个屏幕是为女性设计的。

material: Wood, silver, cloisonné decoration with lapis lazuli, malachite, and coral inlays
description:
Wooden bowls in this form, some lined with metal, were used by Tibetans and Mongolians for drinking tea or fermented liquids, and they were sometimes given as gifts to the Chinese emperor. This elaborate version has a poetic inscription in Chinese that is signed “composed by the Qianlong emperor in the spring of the cyclical year bingxu” [1766]. 

An additional inscription in Manchu attests to the use of both Chinese and Manchu as official languages during this period. The Manchu conquered China in 1644 and founded the Qing dynasty, which lasted until 1911.
credit_line: Gift of the Friends of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, 1978
material_zh: 木、银、景泰蓝装饰，镶嵌青金石、孔雀石和珊瑚
description_zh: 这种形式的木碗，有些内衬金属，被藏人和蒙古人用来喝茶或发酵液体，有时也被作为礼物送给中国皇帝。这个精心制作的版本有一个诗意的中文铭文，上面写着“乾隆皇帝在冰徐年春天创作的”[1766]。满语的另一个铭文证明了这一时期汉语和满语都是官方语言。满族于1644年征服中国，建立了清朝，一直持续到1911年。

description: The Song dynasty emperor Huizong (ruled 1101–26) famously noted in his Treatise on Tea (1107) that the beverage looked its best in bowls decorated with “hare’s fur” markings. The silvery brown effect was the result of excess amounts of iron oxide in the glaze crystallizing during firing.

Each piece bears a number that designates a pattern, by which Howell communicated a customer's choice to his agent in China.

material_zh: 翡翠；翡翠和带金属勺的金属塞
description_zh: 朴素、无装饰的翡翠鼻烟壶在清朝后半叶开始流行，尤其因其自然的颜色、光泽和光滑、抛光的表面而受到赞赏。

material: White jade; sapphire-colored glass and metal stopper with silver spoon
description: Jade snuff bottles are appreciated for their natural colors, often found with subtle brown imperfections that give them a rustic look. This pebble shape fits perfectly in one’s hand, while its smooth, silky surface offers a desirable tactile sensation.
credit_line: Gift of Vera Prock Ransom and Lucille Prock Dean in memory of Lillian W. and Harry A. Prock, 1990
material_zh: 白玉；蓝宝石色玻璃和金属塞，带银勺
description_zh: 玉鼻烟壶因其天然的颜色而受到赞赏，通常会有微妙的棕色瑕疵，使它们看起来很质朴。这种鹅卵石形状非常适合一个人的手，而其光滑、丝滑的表面提供了一种理想的触感。

material: Opaque brown glass overlaid with white and green glass relief decoration; green glass stopper with ivory spoon
description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.


The color combination seen on this bottle is very rare.
dimensions: 1 7/8 x 1 inches (4.8 x 2.5 cm) with stopper
credit_line: Gift of Vera Prock Ransom and Lucille Prock Dean in memory of Lillian W. and Harry A. Prock, 1990
material_zh: 不透明的棕色玻璃，上面覆盖着白色和绿色的玻璃浮雕装饰；带象牙勺的绿色玻璃塞
description_zh:
17世纪末，由烟草磨成粉末并加入芳香物质制成的鼻烟从日本传入中国。中国精英认为这种粉末具有药用价值，最初使用圆柱形药瓶来装这种新的“日本烟草”。不久之后，康熙皇帝（1662-1722年在位）在北京建立了一系列作坊，生产供宫廷使用的小型优质物品，包括鼻烟壶。在帝国的赞助下，瓶子形状、材料和图案的种类急剧增加，工匠们通过添加带有象牙勺的塞子来方便烟草的分配。
在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。
这个瓶子上的颜色组合非常罕见。P

Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. material_zh: 磨砂玻璃，内部涂有水彩装饰，并覆盖有蓝色玻璃浮雕装饰；红色玻璃塞，配象牙色（或白色玻璃）勺子
description_zh:
17世纪末，由烟草磨成粉末并加入芳香物质制成的鼻烟从日本传入中国。中国精英认为这种粉末具有药用价值，最初使用圆柱形药瓶来装这种新的“日本烟草”。不久之后，康熙皇帝（1662-1722年在位）在北京建立了一系列作坊，生产供宫廷使用的小型优质物品，包括鼻烟壶。在帝国的赞助下，瓶子形状、材料和图案的种类急剧增加，工匠们通过添加带有象牙勺的塞子来方便烟草的分配。
在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。P

material: Quartz with naturally occurring snail shell and spotted pattern; tiger's-eye stopper with ivory spoon
description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.
credit_line: Gift of Vera Prock Ransom and Lucille Prock Dean in memory of Lillian W. and Harry A. Prock, 1990

material: Hard-paste porcelain with underglaze enamel and gilt decoration
description: As the first municipal water system in the United States, the Fairmount Waterworks attracted many visitors to Philadelphia in the early nineteenth century. Located on the banks of the Schuylkill River at the foot of Fairmount Park, the waterworks were depicted by numerous American and European artists and subsequently appeared on Chinese export porcelain, as seen on this plate.
credit_line: Purchased with funds contributed by the William B. Dietrich Foundation, 1992

description: This plate was made in China, for export to the English market. The designs in the cartouches on the rim represent the four continents. The figure of Hope in the center supports a shield that bears the Ker family coat of arms. Jane, daughter of Ellis Martin and Elizabeth Ker, married her first cousin, William Ker, about 1790, and this plate is likely from the service made to commemorate their marriage.
credit_line: Gift of William and Ilsa West, 2000
description_zh: 这个盘子是中国制造的，出口到英国市场。轮圈上的漩涡图案代表了四大洲。中间的希望形象支撑着一个带有克尔家族徽章的盾牌。简是埃利斯·马丁和伊丽莎白·科尔的女儿，大约在1790年嫁给了她的堂兄威廉·科尔，这个盘子可能是为了纪念他们的婚姻而制作的。

material: Earthenware with sancai (tricolor) glaze
description: The achievement of early Chinese ceramics is well exemplified by funerary sculptures from the Tang dynasty, when the cosmopolitan empire enjoyed cultural florescence and technological advancement. The imaginative design of this fantastic creature, with its spread wings, protruding manes, and a fearsome dragon head with horns and flames, make it one of the most recognizable guardian figures of the era. It is further enlivened with the celebrated sancai glaze, which incorporates the colors of green, brown, and cream into mesmerizing patterns.
credit_line: The Sallie Crozer Hilprecht Collection, 1929
description_zh: 中国早期陶瓷的成就很好地体现在唐代的葬礼雕塑上，当时这个世界性的帝国享受着文化的繁荣和技术的进步。这个神奇生物的富有想象力的设计，展开的翅膀，突出的鬃毛，以及一个可怕的带角和火焰的龙头，使其成为那个时代最知名的守护者之一。著名的三彩釉使它更加生动，它将绿色、棕色和奶油色融入迷人的图案中。

material: Silk satin weave with silk and gilt strip brocading wefts
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain.
credit_line: Gift of Miss Alice Boney, 1976

material: Silk moiré plain weave with painted watercolor decoration
description: This painted silk fragment decorated with Chinese women, deer, and flowers was said to have been brought from China on the ship Alliance by the Philadelphian Robert Morris in 1788. The ship's cargo consisted of $122,608 worth of teas and silks and $252.74 in sundries for Mrs. Morris. Included as well was a mandarin dress, which Morris presented to the Philadelphia Museum, an institution founded by artist Charles Willson Peale (1741-1827) that held natural history specimens, ethnographic materials, and some portraits by Peale. Painted silks were used both for clothing and for furnishings, including wall coverings. A contrasting selvedge, here in yellow, was characteristic of Chinese trade silks.
credit_line: Gift of Charles Henry Hart, 1897
material_zh: 丝绸云纹平纹织物，带水彩装饰
description_zh: 据说，这块用中国妇女、鹿和鲜花装饰的彩绘丝绸碎片是1788年由费城人罗伯特·莫里斯从中国带到“联盟”号船上的。船上的货物包括价值122608美元的茶叶和丝绸，以及为莫里斯夫人准备的252.74美元的杂货。其中还包括一件中式连衣裙，莫里斯将其赠送给费城博物馆，该博物馆由艺术家查尔斯·威森·皮尔（1741-1827）创立，收藏了自然历史标本、人种学材料和皮尔的一些肖像。彩绘丝绸既用于服装，也用于家具，包括墙纸。对比鲜明的镶边，这里是黄色的，是中国贸易丝绸的特征。

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel and gilt decoration
description: The designs on this piece differ from most late-eighteenth- and nineteenth-century Chinese porcelain made for export to India. Instead of showing motifs popular among Europeans (and favored by Indian Muslims), it depicts Chinese auspicious symbols that include lotuses, peaches, and pomegranates, and has cartouches in the ruyi ("according to [your] will") shape--all of which are meant to wish the owner longevity and good fortune. The Chinese enamelers who copied Arabic script for their customers often misplaced dots and characters, making the Urdu inscriptions difficult to decipher, as on this plate.
credit_line: Gift of Sir David Bruce Duncan and Lady Deana Pitcairn Duncan, 2001
material_zh: 釉上瓷釉和镀金装饰的瓷器
description_zh: 这件作品的设计不同于大多数十八世纪末和十九世纪中国出口到印度的瓷器。它没有展示欧洲人流行的图案（也受到印度穆斯林的青睐），而是描绘了中国的吉祥符号，包括莲花、桃子和石榴，并有如意（“根据你的意愿”）形状的漩涡——所有这些都是为了祝愿主人长寿和好运。为顾客复制阿拉伯文字的中国珐琅匠经常错放点和字符，使乌尔都语铭文难以破译，就像这个盘子上一样。

material: Ink on paper rubbing; mounted on cloth
description: The stone carvings collectively known as the Wu Family shrines are a series of organized pictorial elements and inscriptions forming a tomb discovered in China's Shandong province in 1786.  The stone reliefs however have been studied since the Song Dynasty (960 - 1279) through ink rubbings of their surface decoration and inscriptions.  These rubbings were created by placing paper over the surface of the stone and rubbing the paper with ink, transferring the stone texture to paper.  They are a commonly referenced historical source for the Han Dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE), but they often only selectively represent the stone surface and were in some cases likely altered to depict the "original" stone, eroding their reliability.  Scholars now interpret the rubbings both as representations of the stone carving and pictorial traditions of the Han dynasty in which the stones were created, and also as a revealing look at attitudes towards the study of antiquity in the Song dynasty (and later years) in which the rubbings entered the study of history.
credit_line: Gift of Horace H. F. Jayne, 1924
material_zh: 纸上的墨水摩擦；装在布上
description_zh: 这些石雕统称为吴祠，是1786年在中国山东省发现的一座陵墓中一系列有组织的绘画元素和铭文。然而，自宋代（960-1279）以来，人们就通过拓印其表面装饰和铭文来研究这些石刻。这些拓片是通过在石头表面放置纸张并用墨水擦拭纸张，将石头纹理转移到纸张上而创作的。它们是汉代（公元前206年-公元220年）常用的历史资料，但它们通常只选择性地代表石头表面，在某些情况下可能会被修改以描绘“原始”石头，从而削弱了它们的可靠性。学者们现在将拓片解读为汉代石雕和绘画传统的代表，同时也揭示了宋代（及以后）对古代研究的态度，拓片进入了历史研究。

description: Pagodas are used to mark the burial site of Buddhist relics or the tomb of a Buddhist monk. This carving, modeled after a pagoda, was commissioned by the Qianlong emperor (reigned 1736-95) for a hall located in the imperial park in Beijing. One inscription around the lower middle section of the pagoda says, "I humbly trust myself to Amituofo," and another along the rim of the base states, "Qianlong period of the great Qing dynasty." On the underside of the base is a carved mark that means "Hall of Receiving Light" (Chengguang dian), referring to the building that housed this carving.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944

description_zh: 宝塔被用来标记佛教文物的埋葬地点或佛教僧侣的坟墓。这座雕刻以宝塔为蓝本，是乾隆皇帝（1736-95年在位）为北京皇家公园的一座大厅委托制作的。宝塔中下部有一块铭文写着“我谦卑地相信阿米陀佛”，另一块铭文沿着根据地的边缘写着“大清乾隆时期”。根据地的下面有一个雕刻的标记，意思是“光殿”，指的是容纳这座雕刻的建筑。

material: Porcelain with russet glaze (Ding ware)
description: Ding wares were so popular that kilns around the country copied them. The thinly potted body and lightness of this ovoid bottle distinguish it as an original Ding ware, as opposed to a Cizhou copy, which would be noticeably heavier. According to a Ming dynasty (1368–1644) record, russet-colored Ding wares were rarer than white and so valued more.
credit_line: Gift of Charles H. Ludington from the George Crofts Collection, 1923
description_zh: 丁窑的瓷器非常受欢迎，全国各地的窑都仿制了它们。这个卵形瓶子的薄壶身和轻盈使其成为原始的鼎器，而不是明显更重的磁州仿制品。根据明朝（1368-1644）的记录，赤褐色的丁瓷器比白色的更稀有，因此价值更高。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: This soup plate, part of a larger service, bears the coat of arms of Chadwick quartering Malvesyn and is inscribed on the reverse with the words "Canton in China 24th Jan'y 1791." Although dates appear occasionally on armorial porcelain, this is the only known Chinese service dated in this manner.
credit_line: Purchased with the John D. McIlhenny Fund, 1926
description_zh: 这个汤盘是一个更大的服务的一部分，上面有Chadwick四分卫Malvesyn的徽章，反面刻有“中国广州1791年1月24日”。虽然纹章瓷器上偶尔会出现日期，但这是唯一已知的以这种方式标注日期的中国服务。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: This plate was made in China, for export to the British market.  The plate is part of a dinner service that bears the coat of arms of the Nash family. The star at the bottom of the shield denotes the hereditary line of the third son.

material: Plain weave brocade with cranes and clouds
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940

material_zh: 带有花卉图案的双色斜纹布
description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

description_zh:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

material_zh: 缎面搭配金条、莲花卷轴
description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

description: This plate was made in China, for export to the Islamic market. It is part of an elaborate dessert set made for Azim Jah, a descendent in the Wallajah family and the Nawab of Arcot (ruled 1819 to 1825). The pink pattern is a Chinese copy of a design found on English porcelain.
credit_line: Purchased with the J. W. Lindsay Fund, 1927
description_zh: 这个盘子是中国制造的，出口到伊斯兰市场。这是为瓦拉贾家族和阿科特的纳瓦布（1819年至1825年统治）的后裔阿齐姆·贾制作的精致甜点套装的一部分。粉红色的图案是英国瓷器上发现的一种设计的中国复制品。

Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940

material_zh: 双织丝绸，挂着一排排汉字灯笼
description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

title_zh: 经文封面（一对中的一个）
material: Satin with supplementary weft, lotus scroll
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940

The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

material: Gauze with supplementary weft, dragon and cloud
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

material: Satin damask with lotus scroll
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940
material_zh: 用龙、云和石头编织的平纹锦缎
description_zh:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

material: Porcelain with overglaze enamel and gilt decoration
description: This bowl was made in China, for export to the Islamic market.  It is an example of "clobbered ware," in which preexisting decorations are painted over with new designs. On this piece, a version of the "blossom and nightingale" motif popular in the Persian market is covered with an Urdu inscription honoring a noble woman in her "fourth age." Enamelers sometimes clobbered porcelain so that they could quickly fill special orders.
credit_line: Gift of Mrs. Henry Brinton Coxe, 1928
material_zh: 釉上瓷釉和镀金装饰的瓷器
description_zh: 这个碗是中国制造的，出口到伊斯兰市场。这是一个“破旧器皿”的例子，其中原有的装饰被涂上了新的设计。在这件作品上，波斯市场上流行的“花与夜莺”图案的一个版本上覆盖着乌尔都语铭文，以纪念一位“四岁”的贵族女性。珐琅匠有时会敲打瓷器，以便快速完成特殊订单。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: This scene of the Resurrection of Christ was taken from an illustration in A Written and Visual History, Images of the Old and New Testaments (De Schriftuurlyke Geschiedenissen en Gelykenissen, Van het Oude en Nieuwe Verbond), a book of engravings by Dutch artist Jan Luyken (1649-1712).
credit_line: Bequest of Warner J. Steel, 1937
description_zh: 基督复活的这一场景取自荷兰艺术家扬·吕肯（1649-1712）的版画集《书面和视觉历史，旧约和新约的图像》（De Schriftuurlyke Geschiedenissen en en Gelykenissen，Van het Oude en Nieuwe Verbond）。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: The scene of the Resurrection of Christ shown on this plate was taken from an engraving by Dutch artist Jan Luyken (1649–1712). The engraving, along with others by Luyken, was used to illustrate several editions of the Bible, including one printed in 1734.
credit_line: Bequest of Warner J. Steel, 1937
description_zh: 这个盘子上显示的基督复活的场景取自荷兰艺术家扬·吕肯（1649-1712）的雕刻。这幅雕刻，连同鲁肯的其他雕刻，被用来描绘《圣经》的几个版本，包括1734年印刷的一个版本。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: This plate is painted with a portrait of Martin Luther, the founder of Protestantism, above a depiction of Christ and the disciples—images that derive from an engraving by Franz Brun (German, active c. 1559 to 1596). Brun’s engraving appears on the title page of a Bible published in Amsterdam in 1648 and in a 1734 Bible that includes illustrations by Dutch artist Jan Luyken (1649–1712), whose engravings were also often used as a source for decoration on Chinese export wares.
credit_line: Bequest of Warner J. Steel, 1937
description_zh: 这个盘子上画着新教创始人马丁·路德的肖像，上面画着基督和门徒——这些图像来自弗朗茨·布伦（德语，活跃于1559年至1596年）的雕刻。布伦的雕刻出现在1648年在阿姆斯特丹出版的一本圣经的扉页上，以及1734年的一本《圣经》中，其中包括荷兰艺术家扬·吕肯（1649-1712）的插图，他的雕刻也经常被用作中国出口商品的装饰来源。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: The scene depicted on this plate, taken from an unidentified engraving, may depict the Greek mythology story of the rescue of Andromeda by Perseus. Cepheus, the king of Ethiopia, was forced to sacrifice his beautiful daughter, Andromeda, to a sea monster sent by Poseidon. Perseus, the son of Zeus, had fallen in love with Andromeda and won her hand in marriage by slaying the monster.
credit_line: Bequest of Warner J. Steel, 1937
description_zh: 这个盘子上描绘的场景取自一个身份不明的雕刻，可能描绘了珀尔修斯营救仙女座的希腊神话故事。埃塞俄比亚国王矶斐乌斯被迫将他美丽的女儿仙女座献给波塞冬派来的海怪。宙斯的儿子珀尔修斯爱上了仙女座，并通过杀死怪物赢得了她的婚姻。

material: Hard-paste porcelain with enamel and gilt decoration
description: The Crucifixion scene shown here may have been based on an engraving by Jacques de Gheyn II (Dutch, 1565-1629) after a work by his contemporary Zacharias Dolendo. Gheyn's work was one of a number of engravings of this subject available in the eighteenth century.
credit_line: Bequest of Warner J. Steel, 1937
description_zh: 这里展示的受难场景可能是基于雅克·德·盖恩二世（荷兰语，1565-1629）在其同时代的撒迦利亚·多伦多的一件作品之后的雕刻。Gheyn的作品是十八世纪关于这一主题的众多版画之一。

material: Hard-paste porcelain
description: The decoration on this stand, known as the "Quaker Farmer" pattern, is according to Morris family history based on a drawing made by Mary Hollingsworth Morris (1776-1820).  Mary was married to Israel Morris, the tenth child of "Captain" Samuel Morris, a prominent Philadelphian active in the China Trade.  The claim is supported by a notation on the reverse of the drawing that reads, "The China in Table & Tea Sets was recd Executed calld the Cow China.  Pattern for Set of China copied by Mary H. Morris Jan. 7. 1797."
credit_line: Gift of Dr. John B. Carson, 1976
description_zh: 这个展台上的装饰被称为“贵格会农民”图案，是根据莫里斯家族的历史，基于玛丽·霍林斯沃斯·莫里斯（1776-1820）的一幅画。玛丽嫁给了以色列·莫里斯，他是“上尉”塞缪尔·莫里斯的第十个孩子，塞缪尔·莫里斯是一位活跃于中国贸易的著名费城人。这一说法得到了图纸背面的注释的支持，上面写着：“餐桌和茶具中的瓷器被记录下来，被称为牛瓷器。玛丽·H·莫里斯于1月7日复制的瓷器图案。”。 1797."

material: Plain weave brocade with a part of dragon
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940

credit_line: The Alfred and Margaret Caspary Memorial Gift, 1955

title_zh: 观音的五十三种慈悲表现
material: Woodblock print; ink on paper
description:
Since being introduced to China in the 200s, different forms of the popular Buddhist deity, Guanyin (Sanskrit: Avalokiteshvara), have been created by Chinese devotees and artists. An enlightened being who vowed to remain in this world to help others achieve enlightenment, the compassionate bodhisattva Guanyin can assume different forms in response to individual’s needs. 
This woodblock is part of the book Fifty-Three Compassionate Manifestations of Guanyin, which was originally produced in the early 1600s, and contains images of Guanyin in his many different manifestations. Published to disseminate popular beliefs surrounding this deity, the book encompasses all the well-known forms such as White-robed, Water Moon, and Child-granting Guanyin, and also includes remarkably inventive versions of the deity appearing as the Buddha and even a European gentleman. Rendered in the fine-line (baimiao) technique associated with the celebrated painter of Buddhist figures, Ding Yunpeng (1547-1621), the illustrations are accompanied by an essay by Hu Yingling (1551-1602), a noted scholar who cited various stories demonstrating the efficacy of Guanyin.
The Museum’s nineteen printed images, each now mounted as an album leaf, depicts an image of Guanyin accompanied by a related Chan-Buddhist poem, which differs in composition from most other extant examples printed in the 1700s and 1800s. Unusual also are the many depictions of the Buddhist guardian, Weituo, and inscriptions stating that publication of this book was funded by a Buddhist follower, Hou Huai.
credit_line: Gift of J.W.N. Munthe, 1931
description_zh: 自20世纪20年代传入中国以来，中国的信徒和艺术家创造了不同形式的流行佛教神观音（梵文：观世音）。观音菩萨是一位开悟的人，他发誓要留在这个世界上帮助他人实现开悟，慈悲的菩萨可以根据个人的需求采取不同的形式。这幅木版画是《观音五十三显》一书的一部分，该书最初于17世纪初出版，其中包含了观音在许多不同表现形式中的形象。这本书是为了传播围绕这位神的流行信仰而出版的，它涵盖了所有著名的形式，如白衣、水月和授子观音，还包括以佛陀甚至欧洲绅士的形象出现的神的非常有创意的版本。这些插图采用与著名佛教人物画家丁云鹏（1547-1621）相关的细线（白庙）技术绘制，并附有著名学者胡应龄（1551-1602）的一篇文章，他引用了各种故事来证明观音的功效。博物馆的十九幅印刷图像，每幅现在都被装裱成相册页，描绘了一幅观音像，并附有一首相关的禅宗诗，这首诗的构图与18世纪和19世纪印刷的大多数其他现存作品不同。不同寻常的是，许多对佛教守护者魏陀的描绘，以及铭文，表明这本书的出版是由佛教信徒侯怀资助的。

Unusual also are the many depictions of the Buddhist guardian, Weituo, and inscriptions stating that publication of this book was funded by a Buddhist follower, Hou Huai.

description_zh: 自20世纪20年代传入中国以来，中国的信徒和艺术家创造了不同形式的流行佛教神观音（梵文：观世音）。观音菩萨是一位开悟的人，他发誓要留在这个世界上帮助他人实现开悟，慈悲的菩萨可以根据个人的需求采取不同的形式。这幅木版画是《观音五十三显》一书的一部分，该书最初于17世纪初出版，其中包含了观音在许多不同表现形式中的形象。这本书是为了传播围绕这位神的流行信仰而出版的，它涵盖了所有著名的形式，如白衣、水月和授子观音，还包括以佛陀甚至欧洲绅士的形象出现的神的非常有创意的版本。这些插图采用与著名佛教人物画家丁云鹏（1547-1621）相关的细线（白庙）技术绘制，并附有著名学者胡应龄（1551-1602）的一篇文章，他引用了各种故事来证明观音的功效。博物馆的十九幅印刷图像，每幅现在都被装裱成相册页，描绘了一幅观音像，并附有一首相关的禅宗诗，这首诗的构图与18世纪和19世纪印刷的大多数其他现存作品不同。不同寻常的是，许多对佛教守护者魏陀的描绘，以及铭文，表明这本书的出版是由佛教信徒侯怀资助的。

material: Satin with supplementary weft, buddhist symbols
description:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.
credit_line: Purchased with the John T. Morris Fund from the Carl Schuster Collection, 1940

Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

description_zh:
Buddhist scriptures, or sutras, that are bound accordion-style are often protected with covers composed of paperboard wrapped in decorative silk. Titles and volume numbers appear on strips of paper adhered to the fabric. The use of silk for sutra covers dates back to at least the Tang dynasty (618–907), but most extant examples are from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) and later, when the printing industry grew in China. In some cases, sutra printings were sponsored by the imperial government, resulting in superior editions based on texts from the palace collection.

Complex weaves of lustrous silk, sometimes highlighted by metallic thread, made possible a wide array of patterns and motifs. These include floral scrolls, Buddhist emblems, traditional Chinese symbols of blessings, and Chinese characters with auspicious meanings, all of which are also found on Buddhist ritual vessels and implements made from lacquer, gold, silver, and porcelain. 

In 1940 more than five hundred sutra covers, along with a significant assemblage of other textiles, were acquired from Carl Schuster, who served as assistant curator of Chinese art at the Philadelphia Museum of Art from 1935 to 1937. Schuster’s collection is an important resource for the study of Chinese textiles.

material_zh: 带有珍宝和奖章的灯笼由四朵如意头云组成，位于纳粹党徽之上
description_zh: 手风琴式装订的佛经通常用装饰性丝绸包裹的纸板制成的盖子保护。标题和卷号出现在粘在织物上的纸条上。丝绸用于佛经封面至少可以追溯到唐朝（618-907），但现存的大多数例子都来自明朝（1368-1644）以及后来中国印刷业发展的时期。在某些情况下，佛经印刷是由帝国政府赞助的，从而产生了基于宫廷收藏文本的高级版本。光泽丝绸的复杂编织，有时以金属线为亮点，使各种图案和图案成为可能。这些包括花卉卷轴、佛教标志、中国传统的祝福符号和具有吉祥意义的汉字，所有这些都可以在由漆、金、银和瓷器制成的佛教礼器和器具上找到。1940年，从1935年至1937年担任费城艺术博物馆中国艺术助理馆长的卡尔·舒斯特那里获得了500多个佛经封面，以及大量其他纺织品。舒斯特的藏品是研究中国纺织品的重要资源。

material: Glazed stoneware (Ge ware)
description:
Inscribed on the base (translated): I wonder if someone replaced the incense from last night. Surprisingly, the fragrance can still be smelled until now. The design (of the handles) was taken from the chong fish, and the color is like that of a patterned eel. There is no fire or smoke to begin with, but it seems like there is cloud and mist. After I finished distinguishing the eight transformations and discerning the true nature, how can I tell where the roots of my nose are?

Composed by the Qianlong emperor in the mid-spring of the cyclical year of Bingshen (AD 1776)

This incense burner demonstrates China’s deep reverence of its past. Most likely made during the Ming dynasty, its form is based on a thirteenth- to fourteenth-century Ge ware censer, which itself was modeled after an archaic bronze food offering vessel. The Qianlong emperor (ruled 1736–95) of the Qing dynasty (1644–1911) admired these historical aspects and had his sentiments carved into the base.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944
description_zh: 底座上刻着（翻译）：不知道昨晚有没有人换了香。令人惊讶的是，直到现在，香味仍然可以闻到。（把手）的设计取自冲鱼，颜色就像一条有图案的鳗鱼。起初没有火或烟，但似乎有云和雾。在我区分了八个转变并辨别了真实的本质之后，我怎么知道我的鼻子的根在哪里？乾隆皇帝在炳神（公元1776年）的仲春所作这个香炉体现了中国对过去的深深敬畏。它最有可能是在明朝制造的，其形式基于十三至十四世纪的葛香炉，而葛香炉本身是仿照一个古老的青铜供品器皿制作的。清朝（1644-1911）乾隆皇帝（1736-95年在位）钦佩这些历史方面，并将他的感情刻在了基座上。

material: Porcelain with enamel relief and gilt decoration; green-stained ivory stopper with ivory spoon
description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.

This rare example demonstrates the elegance and splendor of snuff bottles with enameled relief designs on gilded backgrounds. The raised ornamentation and lotus petals around the lower neck play off the characters lian ding, meaning continuous male offspring, and the six petal-shaped panels on the body are decorated with formalized floral scrolls, creating an emblem for longevity by virtue of their elongated, meandering vines.

material_zh: 带有珐琅浮雕和镀金装饰的瓷器；带象牙勺的绿色象牙塞
description_zh:
17世纪末，由烟草磨成粉末并加入芳香物质制成的鼻烟从日本传入中国。中国精英认为这种粉末具有药用价值，最初使用圆柱形药瓶来装这种新的“日本烟草”。不久之后，康熙皇帝（1662-1722年在位）在北京建立了一系列作坊，生产供宫廷使用的小型优质物品，包括鼻烟壶。在帝国的赞助下，瓶子形状、材料和图案的种类急剧增加，工匠们通过添加带有象牙勺的塞子来方便烟草的分配。
在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。这个罕见的例子展示了镀金背景上珐琅浮雕设计的鼻烟壶的优雅和辉煌。四周凸起的装饰和莲花瓣

在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。P

description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.
credit_line: Gift of Major General and Mrs. William Crozier, 1944

material: Opaque white glass overlaid with red glass relief decoration; carnelian and metal stopper with green glass finial; no spoon
description:
Snuff-made of tobacco that is ground into a powdered form and spiced with aromatic substances-was introduced to China from Japan in the late seventeenth century. Chinese elites believed that the powder had medicinal properties, and initially used cylindrical medicine bottles to hold this new "Japanese tobacco." Soon after, the Kangxi Emperor (reigned 1662 - 1722)-known for his fondness for snuff and a devoted patron of the arts-established a series of workshops in Beijing to manufacture small, high-quality objects for court use, including snuff bottles. The repertoire of bottle shapes, materials, and motifs dramatically expanded under imperial patronage, and artisans facilitated the dispensing of the tobacco by adding stoppers with attached ivory spoons.


Snuff bottle production reached aesthetic and technological heights during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor (1736 - 1795), who particularly appreciated the artfulness of the miniature containers. Members of the Qianlong court frequently exchanged the exquisite receptacles as gifts, and by the mid-nineteenth century, snuff bottles had become mandatory items of apparel for Chinese gentlemen and those who aspired to this status. The popularization of these vessels helps account for the many glass bottles produced to resemble jade, agate, quartz, lapis lazuli, and other precious materials: glass snuff bottles were less expensive and a good imitation passed all but the closest scrutiny. Chinese interest in snuff bottles as collectibles continued into the twentieth century, when delicate, inside-painted wares dominated the market.

The delicate overlay decoration shows the legendary demon-queller Zhong Kui pushing a cart of dowry for his sister, who appears on the other side of this bottle. This scene depicts a story in Chinese folklore popularly known as Zhong Kui jia mei (Zhong Kui marrying off his sister), in which mei is a homophone for the word “demon,” conveying the idea of driving evils away.
credit_line: Gift of Eugenia Fuller Atwood, 1977
material_zh: 不透明的白色玻璃上覆盖着红色玻璃浮雕装饰；红玉髓和金属塞子，顶部为绿色玻璃；没有勺子
description_zh:
17世纪末，由烟草磨成粉末并加入芳香物质制成的鼻烟从日本传入中国。中国精英认为这种粉末具有药用价值，最初使用圆柱形药瓶来装这种新的“日本烟草”。不久之后，康熙皇帝（1662-1722年在位）在北京建立了一系列作坊，生产供宫廷使用的小型优质物品，包括鼻烟壶。在帝国的赞助下，瓶子形状、材料和图案的种类急剧增加，工匠们通过添加带有象牙勺的塞子来方便烟草的分配。
在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。精致的叠加装饰显示，传说中的妖师钟馗推着一车嫁妆给妹妹，妹妹出现在瓶子的另一边。这个场景d

material: Agate with carved decoration in high relief; coral stopper; ivory spoon
description: The Daoist God of Longevity usually appears as an aged man with a prominent forehead and a long, flowing beard. Here he is depicted holding a peach, accompanied by a deer and a crane in flight among clouds. These motifs, whether appearing individually or together, convey one’s wish for reaching an old age.
credit_line: Gift of Eugenia Fuller Atwood, 1977
material_zh: 玛瑙，高浮雕雕刻装饰；珊瑚塞；象牙勺
description_zh: 道教的长寿神通常看起来是一个额头突出、胡须飘逸的老人。在这里，他被描绘成手持桃子，伴随着鹿和鹤在云层中飞翔。这些图案，无论是单独出现还是一起出现，都传达了人们对衰老的渴望。

在乾隆皇帝（1736-1795）统治时期，鼻烟壶的生产达到了美学和技术的高度，他特别欣赏微型容器的艺术性。乾隆朝廷的成员经常交换精美的容器作为礼物，到19世纪中叶，鼻烟壶已成为中国绅士和渴望获得这种地位的人的必备服装。这些器皿的普及有助于解释为什么生产出许多类似玉石、玛瑙、石英、青金石和其他珍贵材料的玻璃瓶：玻璃鼻烟壶更便宜，一个好的仿制品几乎通过了最严格的审查。中国人对鼻烟壶作为收藏品的兴趣一直持续到二十世纪，当时精致的内部彩绘制品主导了市场。P

material: Porcelain with underglaze, overglaze enamel, and gilt decoration
description: Samuel Howell, Jr. (c. 1748-1802), a Philadelphia merchant involved in the China trade, used this cup, as well as an assortment of other cups and saucers, to show his customers the patterns available when purchasing a Chinese porcelain service. Each piece bears a number that designates a pattern, by which Howell communicated a customer's choice to his agent in China.
credit_line: Gift of John R. Young, 1986
material_zh: 釉下、釉上、镀金装饰瓷器
description_zh: Samuel Howell，Jr.（约1748-1802年）是一位参与中国贸易的费城商人，他使用这个杯子以及各种其他杯子和碟子向顾客展示购买中国瓷器服务时可用的图案。每件作品都有一个数字，代表一种图案，豪厄尔通过这个图案将客户的选择传达给他在中国的代理商。

description: Vinaya are texts on discipline for Buddhist monastic life. This set contains ten separately bound chapters; nine deal with rules for nuns and the tenth describes those for monks. The frontispiece of this sutra is printed with the delicate ink lines commonly seen in depictions of religious themes in the Ming dynasty. This panoramic view shows the historical Buddha Shakyamuni seated on the lion throne at center with his hands held in the gesture of preaching. He is flanked by large groups of followers, among them the eighteen luohans (disciples of the Buddha) and attending deities from Chinese folk beliefs. All of the figures stand amid swirling clouds, with other celestial deities flying at the top of this image to complete this heavenly assemblage.
credit_line: Purchased with funds contributed by Howard A. Wolf, 1974
material_zh: 缎面辅以金条、鹤、花和珍宝；缎面配金边，牡丹卷轴
type_zh: 书籍/手稿/信息人工制品
description_zh: 戒律是佛教寺院生活的戒律文本。这套包含十个单独装订的章节；其中九条涉及修女的规则，第十条描述了僧侣的规则。这部佛经的扉页上印着明代宗教题材描绘中常见的精致墨线。这幅全景图显示了历史悠久的释迦牟尼佛坐在中央的狮子座上，双手抱着布道的姿势。他身边有一大群追随者，其中包括十八位罗汉（佛陀的弟子）和中国民间信仰中的主神。所有的人物都站在旋转的云层中，其他天神在这张照片的顶部飞行，完成了这一天象的组合。
